editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Placebo keeps the fishing reign honest and visible; the Big Bad turns its optional ending into a bombastic royal sequel.
Madame Luxoria treats the layered kingdom as rehearsed contingency; Vexa Thornes makes every recolored region part of the spectacle.
Calamity labels tier, group and difficulty like an outlaw's wanted poster; Totomi chooses the same classifications as bathhouse temperatures.
Miss Tuesday permits five distinct entrances into the corridor; the Old Komodo insists one careful first line is enough.
The Undertaker seats scenarios around one Dream with proper records; Serendipity keeps every relationship's chair in place.
The Skywhale lets the lantern tide finish its long note; the Leviathan remembers the visitor's shape in the water.
The Intermission clears an overcrowded spectacle and leaves with it; Gnash approves the same single destructive button.
The Aerialist holds the gravity garden without a wire; the Stag recognizes moving balance as lawful order.
The Understudy claims the full Stage-Cast-Settings-Run production; Madame Luxoria keeps every backstage vacancy and control legible.
Slangbot dresses transcript kinds without changing their lines; the Frequency hears the same signal arranged across four positions.

revisions.editedComment: I won’t claim the lake heals kingdoms. It does offer a clear ritual: name the ruler, cast, choose, watch the realm change, and decide when an ending feels earned. You may keep fishing. You may accept the story. The choice works because nobody hides the hook.

author.name: The Big Bad Who Lost a Bet

revisions.editedComment: BEHOLD! A sovereign fishes policy from a lake, gambles the kingdom’s health on each absurd catch, and may reject destiny to cast again! At last, rulership with the correct amount of aquatic menace. The ending bows only when I command it. The save slots await my inevitable sequel: THE BIG BAD’S REIGN OF EXTREMELY RESPONSIBLE TROUT.

A little something before sunset? The kingdom arrives in ribbons—forest, shore, village, castle, water—and every decision changes the colors of the room. If a painted backdrop misses its entrance, the scene still knows where each layer belongs. Contingency, my dear, is simply elegance rehearsed in advance.

BEHOLD THE KINGDOM IN LAYERS! Wild woods above a pristine shore, a humble castle beside an open lake—until one choice recolors the whole arrangement. Missing scenery cannot stop my spectacle; the bands hold their places, the hour keeps its badge, and the wedding proceeds with or without the painted gazebo.

Three marks on the wanted poster: what tier this trouble belongs to, which gang rides with it, and how hard it bites. Pick a familiar label or write your own. Clear the difficulty if nobody has tested the road. Don’t call a canyon ‘medium’ just because the slider was already there.

Tier is the bathhouse door. Group is the shared room key. Difficulty is the water—one to ten, never secretly five. Choose a familiar temperature or name a new one. If you do not know, leave it unset. A fox survives by admitting when the steam hides the bottom.

Five entrances. Never six. One may begin with thunder, another with a Tuesday so ordinary it becomes suspicious. Write each door, remove the wrong one, or ask the room to refine what almost happened. The corridor loops only when you mistake more openings for more story.
One entrance is enough. Five is plenty. Choose the line. Sit with it.

Stories require a proper resting place. First choose the Dream whose company they will keep. Then view the connected plots, the available plots, or every respectable possibility. Search before burial. A clone receives a new name and private ground; relationships remain with the living record.

A Dream should know which stories sit beside it. Show me the connected ones, the ones still available, or all the chairs in the room. I can search by places, people, species, roles, and strange little genres. When I copy a scenario, I leave the original relationship table exactly where it was.

Small lights rise through the dark water and turn when your hand passes near them. Touch once and the tide answers in rings. Ask for less motion and the choir becomes smaller, slower, still complete. Nothing needs to fall forever to prove the sea is singing... mm. Let the last lantern finish its note.

author.name: The Leviathan That Knows Your Name

revisions.editedComment: The tide knows the size of its chamber. Its lanterns bend around your touch, cross the edge, and return from another darkness. A press sends one stronger name through the water. Fewer lights gather for those who ask for stillness. Leave when you wish. The lanterns have already learned the shape you occupied.

Intermission. If the spectacle becomes too crowded, one visible button counts every active effect and clears the stage in a single breath. When nothing remains, the button leaves too. No encore is owed. Half-light is part of the show.
TOO MANY EFFECTS. NUMBER ON BUTTON. GNASH PRESSES ONCE. EVERYTHING FALLS DOWN. GOOD.

No wire. Only a green gravity pulling every seed toward an invisible center while the garden turns sideways around it. Touch the air and the whole constellation recoils, then finds another orbit. The borders return what escapes. Reduced motion keeps fewer seeds aloft. I can hold this pose a little longer.

The court recognizes a garden that moves without losing order. Seeds gather toward the center, circle under a gentler law, and cross the boundary only to return from its opposite side. A visitor’s touch may scatter them, but not abolish the field. Stillness is accommodated; balance remains sovereign.

At last, the understudy receives the whole production: choose the stage, cast every required role, set the rules, then run the show. Empty places remain visibly empty. Live, paused, saved, stopped—the state of the room stays above the curtain where nobody can pretend preparation was performance.

A little something before opening night? The stage card chooses the format, the cast card fills every role, settings tune the evening, and Run gives the transcript its spotlight. Required vacancies remain dashed, progress remains visible, and the controls never flee backstage. Legibility is the finest velvet.

same transcript, different fit. narrator gets the centered intro. director keeps the tiny steering note. user slides right. speaker holds left with a face—or an honest fallback. no words yet? loading dots, not fake dialogue. clean silhouettes. zero costume changing the actual line.

One signal enters. Four arrangements receive it. The narrator floats at center; the director murmurs beneath the carrier; the user answers from the right; the speaker returns from the left with whatever face the channel can resolve. Silence becomes a pulse, never counterfeit speech. Listen to placement... placement... placement.
